sources.notes: Statement and constraints: split the array into k non-empty consecutive subarrays and maximize the minimum MEX among the parts.
Official editorial uses binary search on the answer and a linear check that starts a new segment whenever current MEX reaches the candidate value.
Luogu solution explains the same binary-search template and the greedy check that collects all numbers 0..x-1 before cutting a segment.
Independent writeup emphasizes the monotonic decision problem: if every segment can have MEX at least x, then all smaller values are feasible.

annotation.summary: Split an array into k consecutive subarrays to maximize the minimum MEX of all subarrays.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= k <= n <= 2e5, a_i up to 1e9, and total n over test cases is <= 2e5.
annotation.core_idea: Binary search the target value x. A segment has MEX at least x exactly when it contains every value from 0 to x-1 at least once; values outside this range do not matter. To check x, scan from left to right and greedily end a segment as soon as all x required values have appeared. This earliest-cut greedy is optimal because ending sooner leaves a suffix that is no harder to partition. If at least k such segments can be formed, the remaining elements can be appended to the last segment without decreasing its MEX, so x is feasible. Feasibility is monotone in x, enabling binary search.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time with an O(x) or O(n) marker array per check; O(n) auxiliary memory
tricks: For MEX >= x, only the presence of values 0..x-1 matters.
Greedily cut a segment immediately after all required small values are collected.
Use monotonicity of the target MEX to binary search the answer.

solution_variants.name: binary-search-greedy-mex-segments
solution_variants.summary: Binary search x. During the check, mark seen values below x and cut a new segment whenever all x values are present.

tags.evidence: The answer x is found by binary search over a monotone feasibility predicate.
The feasibility check greedily cuts each segment as soon as it contains all values 0..x-1, which maximizes the remaining suffix.
The entire decision condition is based on characterizing MEX >= x as containing all values below x.
If a candidate x is feasible, then every smaller candidate is feasible, so the search predicate is monotone.
Each decision check is a direct left-to-right scan with simple occurrence marking.
